Resolved
Resolved,
That the said Bill do pass,
Ordered,
That Mr. Nevill, Mr. Read, Mr. Fisher, and Mr. Ladd, do carry the said Bill to the Council for their Concurrence.

The House adjourn'd till two P. M.

The House met.

Mr. Nevill reported, that Mr. Read, Mr. Fisher, Mr. Ladd, and himself, delivered the Bill with them entrusted to the Council.

Mr. Smith from the Committee appointed to prepare the Draught of an Address to his Excellency; reported that they had prepared the same, which by leave he read and delivered in at the Table; which being again read, was agreed to by the House, and ordered to be engross'd.

The engross'd Address to his Excellency, was read and compared.
Ordered,
That Mr. Speaker do Sign the same.
Ordered,
That Mr. Smith, and Mr. Johnston, do wait on his Excellency and desire to know when he will be waited on by the House with their Address.

Mr. Smith reported, that Mr. Johnston, and himself delivered the above Message to his Excellency, who was pleased to say he would send for the House to-morrow Morning.

The House adjourn'd till to-morrow Morning nine o'Clock.

Tuesday December 23, 1755.

The House met.

A Written Message from his Excellency as follows.

'Gentlemen of the General Assembly.
YESTERDAY Colonel Schuyler laid before me the State of the Regiment of this Province, which I herewith send you, and whereby you will see what Arms and other Things they want to be supplied with, for their proceeding on the Campaign intended in the ensuing Year; and as I doubt not you will find them absolutely necessary for the Service, and that you will now take effectual Care to supply them.'
Elizabeth-Town, Council Chamber,
December 23, 1755.
J. BELCHER.
The following is an Account of the present State of the Regiment of this Province, laid before his Excellency by Col. Schuyler, viz.

"Give me leave to represtent to your Excellency the State of the New-Jersey Regiment under my Command, viz.

"The lost of Muskets by Accidents and Desertion 100
"Cartridge Boxes. ditto ditto 198
"Bayonets. ditto ditto 293
"Kettles mostly worn out.
"Tents unfit for another Campaign.

"Have not as yet the Muster Rolls of the last Payment from the Captains, so cannot give your Excellency a just Return of the Men.
December 22 1755. PETER SCHUYLER.

To his Excellency JONATHAN BELCHER Esqr.

Resolved,
That Hendrick Fisher, and Jacob Spicer, Esqrs. Commissioners, do supply the Forces in the pay of this Colony, under Colonel Schuyler with all the Necessaries they may want, as near as may be conforming themselves to the Directions of the A& entitled, An Act for making Current Fifteen Thousand Pounds in Bills of Credit for the further supply and pay of the Forces lately raised in this Colony under the Command of Colonel Peter Schuyler, and for directing the manner how the said Fifteen Thousand Pounds shall be junk, but where the said A& does not provide for keeping up and maintaining the full Quantity of Guns, Cartridge Boxes, Bayonets, Kettles and Tents, they are to supply them. Provided always, that due care be taken that the Intention of the Law may be complied with, in deducting from the Soldiers Wages, the Prices of any thing that they may negligently or wilfully waste.

Resolved,
That whatever Necessaries the said Hendrick Fisher and Jacob Spicer shall Supply the said Forces with, while they are on the Frontiers of this Colony, or repairing there, shall be allowed in their Accounts when laid before the General Assembly.

Whereas it seems likely that Money for the Purposes intended by the A& passed this sitting, will be wanted before it can be got ready.

Resolved,
That upon Application made to the Treasurers of this Colony, or either of them, they, or either of them, shall pay to John Stevens, John Johnston, John Wetherill, and Jonathan Hampton, Esqrs' or either of them, such Sum or Sums as they may respectively want in Con-
sequence of the Directions of the Act of Assembly past at this Sitting, out of the Money made Current by the Act for emitting the last Fifteen Thousand Pounds in Bills of Credit, and to replace the same out of the Money to come into their Hands by Virtue of the Act pass'd this Sitting.

Ordered,
That Mr. Johnston and Mr. Wetherill do carry the above Resolves to the Council for Concurrence.

A Message from his Excellency by Mr. Secretary,

"Mr. Speaker,
"His Excellency is in the Council Chamber ready to receive the Address of the House."

Whereupon Mr. Speaker left the Chair, and with the House went to wait on his Excellency, and being returned, Mr. Speaker resumed the Chair, and reported, that the House had waited on his Excellency, and delivered their Address in the following Words.

To his Excellency JONATHAN BELCHER, Esq; Capt. General and Governor in Chief, in and over his Majesty's Province of New-Jersey, and Territories thereon depending in America, Chancellor and Vice Admiral in the same, &c.

The Humble ADDRESS of the Representatives of the said Province in General Assembly met.

May it please your Excellency,

WE His Majesty's dutiful and loyal Subjects, the Representatives of the Colony of New-Jersey, in General Assembly conven'd, being compassionately affected at the Barbarities committed by the Indian Enemy on our Neighbours of Pennsylvania, cannot but esteem this Province obliged by your Excellency's Care, in giving us this Opportunity of providing for the Security of our Frontiers against the unrelenting Cruelties of so savage an Enemy.

As by the Accounts we have received, it appears that Pennsylvania is depopulated for a considerable Distance; that many of her Inhabitants are Murdered, and some with most aggravating Marks of Inhumanity: That many fine Plantations and Improvements are laid waste, and several of them even within the View of our People on the Frontiers of this Province, who are thereby become a Frontier themselves. It is therefore not to be wondered at, that they are in Confusion, and likely to leave their Plantations if something is not done for their Relief: On which Occasion, this House has received a considerable Number of Petitions from many Parts of the Province sign'd
signed, by an usual Number of Freeholders, praying that something may be done on the present Emergency. All which having been duly considered, together with the Inconvenience of making an addition at this Time to the heavy Taxes already laid on the Inhabitants of this Colony, we have, after due Deliberation, by the Bill sent to the Council, for making current Ten Thousand Pounds, made such Provision, as with the Blessing of divine Providence, we hope will give the desired Relief.

By Order of the House
Assembly-Chamber December 22, 1755.
ROBERT LAWRENCE. Speaker.

Mr. Johnson reported, that Mr. Wetherill, and himself delivered the Resolves of this House, with them entrusted, to Mr. Ogden one of the Gentlemen of the Council.

A Message from the Council by Mr. Altfeld, acquainting the House, that the Council have pass'd the Bill, entitled, An Act for making Current Ten Thousand Pounds in Bills of Credit, to enable the Inhabitants of this Colony, to protect and defend the Frontiers, &c. without any Amendments.

The House adjourn'd till two P. M.

The House met.

Ordered,
That Mr. Dehart, and Mr. Bradberry, do wait on the Council and desire to know if they have any Business before them; if not, that this House purposes to apply to his Excellency for a Dismission.

Mr. Dehart reported, that Mr. Bradberry, and himself delivered the above Message to the Council, who was pleased to Answer, they had no Business before them.

Ordered,
That Mr. Smith, and Mr. Johnston, do wait on his Excellency, and acquaint him, the Council have informed this House, they have no Business before them, and to desire his Excellency would be pleased to pass the Bill sent up to him and to dismiss the House, if he has nothing further to lay before them.

Mr. Smith, reported that Mr. Johnston, and himself delivered the above Message to his Excellency, who was pleased to say, the House should have a Message from him soon.

Mr. Secretary, brought from his Excellency, the Resolves of this House with the Council's Concurrence, and his Excellency's Assent thereto, in the form following.
"Assembly, Chamber, December, 23: 1755.

"Resolved.
"That Hendrick Fisher, and Jacob Spicer, Esqrs; Commissioners, do supply the Forces in the pay of this Colony under Col. Schuyler, with all the Necessaries they may want, as near as may be conforming themselves to the Directions of the Act, entitled, An Act, for making current Fifteen Thousand Pounds in Bills of Credit, for the further supply and pay of the Forces lately raised in this Colony, under the Command of Col. Peter Schuyler, and for directing the manner how the said Fifteen Thousand Pounds shall be sunk, but where the said Act, does not provide for keeping up and maintaining the full Quantity of Guns, Cartridge Boxes, Bayonets, Kettles, and Tents, they are to supply them. PROVIDED ALWAYS, that due Care be taken that the Intention of the Law, may be complied with, in deducting from the Soldiers Wages, the Price of any Thing that they may negligently or wilfully waste.

"Resolved,
"That whatever Necessaries the said Hendrick Fisher and Jacob Spicer, shall supply the said Forces with, while they are on the Frontiers of this Colony or repairing there, shall be allowed in their Accounts when laid before the General Assembly.

"Whereas it seems likely that Money for the Purposes intended by the Act passed this sitting, will be wanted before it can be got ready.

"Resolved,
"That upon Application made to the Treasurers of this Colony or either of them, they or either of them shall pay to John Stevens, John Johnston, John Wetherill, and Jonathan Hampton, Esqrs. or either of them, such Sum or Sums as they may respectively want in Consequence of the Directions of the Act of Assembly passed at this sitting, out of the Money made Current by the Act, for emitting the last Fifteen Thousand Pounds in Bills of Credit, and to replace the same out of the Money to come into their Hands by Virtue of the Act passed this sitting.

"Ordered,
"That Mr. Johnston, and Mr. Wetherill, do carry the above resolves, to the Council for Concurrence."

"By Order of the House,
"ROBERT LAWRENCE, Speaker.

"Council Chamber, December 23, 1755. Concurred."

"ANDREW JOHNSTON, Speaker.

"December 23, 1755. I Assent to the above Resolves."
J. BELCHER.
A Message from Excellency by his Clerk as follows,

'Gentlemen of the General Assembly.

'His Excellency General Shirley having by his Letter of the 18th Instant informed me, that it would be greatly for his Majesty's Service in the ensuing Campaign if his Hands could be strengthened by an Augmentation of the Regiment of this Colony, under the Command of Colonel Schuyler, I would have you exert Yourselves on the Occasion as far as the Circumstance of the Colony will admit, as His Majesty proposes to do every thing for your Defence, and that I may be able to give the Commander in Chief of his Majesty's Forces, Information of what Augmentations from this Colony may be depended on, I recommend it to your House, (with whom all these Supplies take Rise) the making a Provision for raising them when they shall be required.

'As the People of this Colony are greatly uneasy at the Neighbourhood of any Indian Families, and suspect the whole of them, I must apply to you to provide for the Maintenance of such of them as shall be committed to the Goals, either on Suspicion, or to secure them from the Outrages of the People.

'The Distresses of the unhappy Sufferers on the Frontiers are doubtless very great, and may be attested with the Ruin of many Families, and I hope you will make some conditional Provision for such as cannot support themselves, as they cannot be regularly relieved by any Law now in Being.

'The seasonable Provision which this Province made for the Supply of His Majesty's regular Troops through this Province, served two very good Purposes, as it expedited their march and prevented them from the Necessity of supplying themselves with Carriages and other things, in a way which would have been ungrateful to themselves and greatly distressing to the People of the Colony. I cannot omit inserting on this Occasion, the handsome mention which is made of that Provision, in a Letter I had the Pleasure of receiving from the Honorable Colonel Dunbar, the commanding Officer of those Troops, in the following Words, viz.

'Sir, It gave me the greatest Concern, I could not when in your Government have the Honour of waiting on your Excellency, to make my most grateful Acknowledgments, for the great Civilities the Troops under my Command received in their march thro' your Government from every Body where we pass'd from Trenton to Amboy.

'I earnestly recommend these Interesting Matters to your Consideration,
that by providing for them at this Time, I may not be obliged to call you together sooner than the General Business of the Government would incline me to do

Elizabeth Town, Council Chamber,
December 23 1755.

J. BELCHER.

On a Motion made and the Question put, whether his Excellency be desired by Proclamation to restrain the Indians inhabiting this Province, to the Counties where they recide? it passed in the Negative.

<table>
  <tr>
    <th>Nays</th>
    <th>Nays</th>
    <th>Yeas</th>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Mr. Stevens,<br>Mr. Johnston,<br>Mr. Nevill,<br>Mr. Holmes,<br>Mr. Bradberry,<br>Mr. Smith,</td>
    <td>Mr. Newbold,<br>Mr. Paxson,<br>Mr. Clement,<br>Mr. Hancock,<br>Mr. Miller,<br>Mr. Spicer,</td>
    <td>Mr. Wetherill,<br>Mr. Fisher,<br>Mr. Huggeland,<br>Mr. Vreeland,<br>Mr. Vangelon,<br>Mr. Read,<br>Mr. Ladd,<br>Mr. Tard,<br>Mr. Middagh,</td>
  </tr>
</table>

The House taking His Excellency's Message into their Consideration;

Ordered,
That Mr. Read and Mr. Stevens do wait on his Excellency, and in Answer to his Message of this Day, inform him, that as to the Application which General Shirley makes to this Colony, for an Augmentation of the Troops under Colonel Schuyler: This House would cheerfully give the greatest Assistance to the Forces his Majesty has Occasion to employ to defend his Territories form Encroachments of a dangerous Neighbour, but we hope to be entirely excused from augmenting our Regiment, as we are at this Time threatened with Invasions from a barbarous and cruel Enemy, and have been under a Necessity of raising a large Number of Troops, and maintaining them in Garrisons, in order to prevent the Destruction, or total Desertion, of the Frontries; and request his Excellency, to return the thanks of this House to General Shirley, for his favourable Sentiments of this Colony, and to assure him, that we esteem ourselves happy in the Appointment his Majesty has been pleased to make of a Commander in Chief of his Troops, as the Colonies in General have the highest Opinion of the Abilities and Integrity of the Gentleman now employed, and his known Attachment to his Majesty's Honour and the Interest of the American Plantations, we hope will induce every Colony to exert themselves to act in an Expedition under so good a Direction. That as it appears that some of the Indians on our Borders have taken up the Hatchet, it is necessary that extream Caution be used with regard to these Inhabiting this Province who
who if any of them shou'd be taken up and on Proof or well grounded Suspicion of being Enemies, shou'd be committed to any of the Goals, we are of Opinion they shou'd be maintained there in the same manner as other Persons charged on criminal Actions now are, and if it shou'd to happen that any County shou'd be over barthened, we doubt not, but when regular Accounts thereof are laid before the General Assembly they will duly consider them. And that as to such of the poor Inhabitants on the Frontires, who are now in distrefs, the Commissary appointed by the Act pass'd this Session, has power to adjust and pay all Persons accounts who have advanced any thing to the Service of the Troops sent there by his Excellency's Direction; and we doubt not if there is any distres that he has not Power to relieve, they will have their due weight with this, or some future General Assembly when matters are settled, so that it may be properly known, who are real Objects of publick Charity. And as we have been informed many of the poor Women and Children from Pensylvania, have been obliged to fly for succour among our Inhabitants; we are of Opinion, that the Commissary shou'd get attested Proofs of the charge that our People have been at for their Maintenance, and to lay them before his Excellency, whose Interposition with that Government on behalf of those People, we doubt not will have a good Effect towards reimbursing such Expences; and we believe it will be necessary that they should be safely and speedily conducted into some part of Pensylvania, where it may be most agreeable to them.

The House adjourn'd till to-morrow Morning nine o'Clock.

Wednesday December 24, 1755.

The House met.

Mr. Read reported, that Mr. Stevens, and himself delivered the Message of the House of Yesterday to his Excellency.

Resolved, Nemine Contradicenti.

That this House, will at their next sitting for doing the publick Business of the Colony, make Provision to defray the expence attending any Persons Trouble, that shall be employed by the Governor, in informing the Friend Indians, inhabitting this Province of the Danger there is in going to the Northern Frontiers, and to desire them to forbear going into that, or any other dangerous Place.

Ordered,
That Mr. Smith, and Mr. Newbold, do wait on his Excellency with the above resolve.

An Account was laid before the House by Petrus Decker, Esq; relating to his taking Indian Prisoners, and bringing them to Elizabeth Town
Town, which was read and referred to further Consideration, at their passing the next Support Bill.

Mr. Smith reported, that Mr. Newbold, and himself, performed the above Order of the House.

A Message from his Excellency by his Clerk.

Mr. Speaker,
"His Excellency requires the Attendance of the House in the Council Chamber immediately."

Whereupon Mr. Speaker left the Chair and with the House went to wait on his Excellency who was pleased to give his Assent to the following Bill, enacting the same; viz,

An Act for making Current Ten Thousand Pounds in Bills of Credit, to enable the Inhabitants of this Colony, to protect and defend their Frontiers, from any Invasion which may be made upon the same by the Enemy, &c.

And then to adjourn'd the General Assembly, to the 22d Day of January next.

BY Virtue of an Order of the House, I do appoint William Bradford of Philadelphia, to print these Votes.

ROBERT LAWRENCE, Speaker.
VOTES
AND
PROCEEDINGS
OF THE
General ASSEMBLY
OF THE
Province of NEW-JERSEY,
At a SESSION held at Elizabeth-Town, by Adjournment, March 9th, 1756, and continued till the 16th Day of the same Month, and then Adjourned to the 7th Day of April following.

PHILADELPHIA:
Printed by WILLIAM BRADFORD, Printer to the Province of New-Jersey.
VOTES
AND
PROCEEDINGS
OF THE
GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF 1795
IN MASSACHUSETTS
VOTES OF THE General ASSEMBLY

ELIZABETH-TOWN, Tuesday March 9, 1756.

PURSUANT to his Excellency's Adjournments of the General Assembly to this Day, the House met.

Ordered,
That Mr. Fisher, and Mr. Smith, do wait on his Excellency and acquaint him, that a sufficient Number of Members to proceed on Business are met, and are ready to receive any thing he shall please to lay before them.

Mr. Fisher reported, that Mr. Smith, and himself delivered the above Message to his Excellency, who was pleased to say, he would send for the House immediately.

A Message from his Excellency by Mr. Secretary.
'Mr. Speaker,
'His Excellency requires the Attendance of the House in the Council Chamber immediately.

Whereupon Mr. Speaker left the Chair, and with the House went to wait on his Excellency. And being returned, Mr. Speaker resumed the Chair, and reported, that the House had waited on his Excellency, who was pleased to make a Speech to the Council, and House of Assembly, of which Mr. Speaker said he had, to prevent Mistakes, obtained a Copy, which was Ordered to be read, and it was read accordingly, and is as follows.

'Gentlemen of the Council, and of the General Assembly.
'I Know of no Time since my Arrival with the Honour of his Majesty's Commission, for the Government of this Province, that has so much demanded our united Deliberations for the Defence, Protection and Safety of this and the Neighbouring Provinces, as this
critical Conjuncture doth; for the Year seems to be opening with the Expectation of great and extraordinary Events, which may probably shew us whether his Majesty is to maintain his just Rights and Dominions in North America, and his Subjects their Religious Liberties and Properties, or whether the perfidious French with their Savage Allies, are to go on in their cruel Murders and Depredations, and finally drive the King's good People into the Sea.

In faithfulness to his Majesty's Honour and Service, and in a tender care of his good Subjects, committed to my Charge, I have given those Things my greatest Attention and Consideration, and would now lay before you several Things, which being put into Execution, may by the Blessing of God, prove preventive of the Evils we have too much reason to fear. And first of all I must mention with gratitude to Almighty God, and to the King, his Majesty's great Goodness, in laying before his Parliament, the State of the Colonies; and also a grateful Sense of his Parliament in the kind Assurances they have given him, to do every Thing on their Part for the Protection and Defence of these Colonies, and for putting a Stop to the further unjust Encroachments of the Enemy.

In the next Place, I now lay before you, the Minutes of a Council of War, lately held at New-York, by his Excellency General Shirley, and which I commit to you, depending on your Honour, that they shall not be divulged; (it is an ancient and wise Proverb, that Secrecy is the Soul of Business) and by these you will see the Plan of Operation, and the Quotas of Men intended to be raised, by the several Colonies, for his Majesty's Service in the ensuing Campaign; and when you consider the Strength of the Enemy, at the several Places to be attack'd, I believe you will think it reasonable and necessary, that this Province should furnish their additional Proportion of Men in Conjunction with the other Colonies.

Which Matters being duly weighed, I hope you will with much Alacrity make up the Regiment of this Province to seven Hundred and Fifty Men, and which you may depend will set you in a very favourable light with his Majesty.

I now also communicate to you, Extracts of a Letter of 2d of January last which I have received from the Honourable Mr. Dinwiddie, Governor of Virginia, by which you will see the vigilant and wise Conduct of that Gentleman for his Majesty's Service, and for the Defence and Safety of the Southern Colonies, in so far bringing the Cherokee Indians into an Alliance and into the Interest of the Crown of Great-Britain, and which may be of an extraordinary good tendency for Success in the ensuing Campaign, and this I hope will further stimulate you to an Augmentation of the Troops of this Province.

Having
Having thus laid before you several Matters relating to the Common Cause of the Colonies, I now recommend to you, your especial Care of the Protection and Safety of this his Majesty's Province, into which since our last meeting, the Bears of the Wilderness have made an Incurison; and as the Year Advances we have reason to believe they will come in great Numbers upon our Frontiers; therefore in duty to his Majesty and in a kind and just Concern for his People, I think we ought immediately to send Commissioners to the Colonies of Pennsylvania, and New-Tork, in order to Act unitedly against the French and Indians, And I herewith give you the Extracts of a Letter of 4th Instant, which I have received from his Excellency Sir Charles Hardy, Governor of New-Tork, which sets the Affairs of the Colonies in so particular and clear a light, as I think must convince you of the Necessity of the Union proposed.

And to add to the Things I have mentioned, I most earnestly recommend to you to listen to the Cries and Expostulations of our poor Brethren on the Frontiers to be protected under their present distresses; and which you will find in a great Number of Petitions, signed by more than a Thousand Hands, which I shall order to be laid before you. And before I finish on this Head I think proper to say to you, that for the more effectually intimidating and driving the Enemy from our Frontiers, it would be the wisest and cheapest method to pass an Act for giving Premiums on Indian Scalps and Captives, as the Massachusetts Government have done; an Extract of whose Resolves in this Matter shall be delivered you.

Gentlemen of the General Assembly,
These Things of so great Importance to his Majesty's Service and Interest, being laid before you in so particular a Manner, I shall not doubt your voting a sufficient Sum for carrying the several Articles into Execution, considering at the same Time, that the Season of the Year admits of no delay therein.

And as your Constituents signify to you, not only their willingness, but their cheerful desire to be taxt to a sufficient Provision for accomplishing these Points, it will therefore be very severe to withhold from them the use of their own Estates, which they would have employed for saving the Lives of themselves, their Wives and Children and their Estates from falling into the Hands of the perfidious French with their cruel Allies, the Savage and Barbarous Indians.

Gentlemen of the Council, and of the General Assembly,
I shall heartily rejoice in your coming into the best Conclusions in these Affairs for advancing his Majesty's Interest and the future lasting Peace and Welfare of this Province.

Elizabeth-Town, Council Chamber,
March 9, 1756.

J. BELCHER.
His Excellency by Mr. Secretary laid before the House, the Letters and Papers referred to in his Speech, which were also read.

Ordered,
That his Excellency's Speech have a second reading to-morrow morning.

The House Adjourn'd till to-morrow Morning nine o'Clock.

Wednesday March 10, 1756.

The House met.

According to Order, his Excellency's Speech was read the second Time, and with the Letters, Petitions and Papers therein referred to, was committed to a Committee of the whole House.

The House accordingly resolved itself into a Committee of the whole House on his Excellency's Speech, and Matters aforefaid. After some Time spent therein, Mr. Speaker resumed the Chair, and Mr. Hancock, Chairman of the Committee, reported, that the Committee had made some Progress in the Matters to them referred, and desired leave to sit again. To which the House agreed.

The House adjourn'd till two P. M.

The House met.

A Petition was presented to the House, setting forth the Inconvenience that the Bergen Forces that goes to defend our Frontiers, labour under, by living so remote from the Pay-master of said Forces, desiring that a Pay-master may be appointed in said County, for the Payment of their Forces, which was read, and ordered a second reading.

A Petition of Captain Vanbuskirk, of Bergen County, was presented to the House setting forth, that he had Marched with a Company of Men to the Defence of the Frontiers of this Province on the River Delaware, and had there erected a Fort and two Block Houses, praying leave to bring in a Bill for obtaining Payment for their Time from the Government, which was read, and Ordered a second reading.

The House again resolved itself into a Committee of the whole House on his Excellency's Speech, and Matters aforefaid. After some Time spent therein, Mr. Speaker resumed the Chair, and Mr. Hancock, Chairman of the Committee, reported, that the Committee had gone through the said Speech, and come to the following Resolutions, which by leave he reported as follows.

Resolved,
That a humble Address be presented to his Excellency in answer to his favourable Speech. To which the House agreed.
Resolved,
That this Colony will Act in Conjunction with the Colonies of New-York, and Pennsylvania, for the attacking the Settlements of the Indian Enemy, in Captivating their Wives and Children, in order to bring them to reason, and to secure a lasting Peace.

And on the Question whether the House will at this Time agree thereto or not? it pass'd in the Affirmative.

Yeas.                Yeas.                Nays.                Nays.
Mr. Stevens,          Mr. Vreeland,         Mr. Holmes,           Mr. Hancock,
Mr. Nevill,           Mr. Vangeson,         Mr. Smith,            Mr. Miller,
Mr. Dehart,           Mr. Read,             Mr. Newbold,          Mr. Leaming,
Mr. Fisher,           Mr. Tard,             Mr. Paxson,           Mr. Spicer,
Mr. Hoggland,         Mr. Middah,           Mr. Clement,

Resolved,
That 250 Volunteers be raised, in lieu of those already on the Frontiers of this Colony, for the future Defence of said Frontiers, and on the Question, whether the House agree thereto or not? it pass'd in the Affirmative.

Yeas.                Yeas.                Nays.                Nays.
Mr. Stevens,          Mr. Vreeland,         Mr. Smith,            Mr. Leaming,
Mr. Nevill,           Mr. Vangeson,         Mr. Newbold,          Mr. Spicer,
Mr. Holmes,           Mr. Read,             Mr. Paxson,           Mr. Clement,
Mr. Dehart,           Mr. Ladd,             Mr. Hancock,          Mr. Miller,
Mr. Fisher,           Mr. Tard,             Mr. Middagh,

Resolved,
That 200 of the said Volunteers do Act in Conjunction with the Colonies of New-York, and Pennsylvania, to make an Army of 1000 Men, if so many is necessary; or if a less number shall be deemed sufficient for that Service, this Colony will support one fifth Part of the said Army, which is to be employed in pursuing the Indian Enemy into their retreats, and captivating them, their Wives and Children. To which the House agreed.

Resolved,
That the above Proportion is greater than this Colony of Right ought to bear, and that nothing but the hearty Zeal of the Inhabitants in the Common Cause, could induce this Committee to take upon the Colony so great a Proportion, and also to prevent any Colour of Complaint of the other Colonies concern'd in the Union, and to accelerate the Enterprize. To which the House agreed.

Ordered,
That Mr. Read, Mr. Smith, Mr. Tard, Mr. Leaming, and Mr. Hancock,
Hancock, be a Committee to prepare the Draught of an Address to his Excellency.

Ordered,
That Mr. Nevill, Mr. Fisher, Mr. Stevens, Mr. Johnson, and Mr. Dehart, be a Committee to bring in a Bill for the several Purposes aforesaid.

The House Adjourn'd till to-morrow morning nine o'Clock.

Thursday March 11 1756.

The House met.

A Petition was presented to the House from John Ogden, and Uzal Ogden, Esqrs. setting forth, that they have at a great Expence erected a Furnace at Ringwood, which is now in the utmost Danger of being destroyed by the Indian Enemy, and thereby a valuable Branch of Trade totally defeated; and that they are about building a Block-House to Guard the said Works and Inhabitants thereabouts, praying the House to afford them Assistance of Men and Arms to defend them against the said Indians, which was read, and Ordered a second reading.

A Petition was presented to the House from a Number of the Inhabitants, living in the Northwestmost Part of Sussex County, setting forth the Danger they are in by reason of the Indian Enemy, desiring that they may be employed by the Government in defending that Part of the Frontiers, on the Allowance already provided for that Purpose, which was read, and Ordered a second reading.

Mr. Nevill from the Committee appointed for that Purpose, brought in a Bill, entitled, An Act for raising two Hundred and fifty able Bodied free Men Volunteers, &c. which was read and committed to a Committee of the whole House,

The House accordingly resolved itself into a Committee of the whole House, on the aforementioned Bill: After some Time spent therein, Mr. Speaker resumed the Chair, and Mr. Hancock, Chairman of the Committee, reported, that the Committee had gone through the said Bill, and made several Amendments thereto, which by leave he read, and delivered in at the Table, where the same being again read, was agreed to by the House.

Ordered,
That the said Bill as amended be engross'd.

The House adjourn'd till to-morrow morning nine o'Clock.
Friday, March 12, 1756.

The House met.

Mr. Smith, from the Committee appointed for that Purpose 'bro't in the Draught of an Address to his Excellency, which was read, and Ordered a second reading.

The House adjourn'd to two P. M.

The House met.

Several Petitions were presented to the House from the County of Morris, and Places adjacent, setting forth the Danger their Settlements are likely soon to be expoled to from the Indian Enemy, desiring Protection from this Colony; which were read, and ordered a second reading.

The engrossed Bill entitled, An Act for raising two Hundred and Fifty able Bodied Free Men, as Volunteers, to protect and defend the Frontiers of this Colony from any Invasions which may be made upon the same by the Enemy, in lieu of the Militia already stationed upon the said Frontiers; as also for subjecting two Hundred of the said Volunteers, to act in Conjunction with the Colonies of New-York and Pennsylvania, to make up an Army of One Thousand Men, or one fifth Part of such an Army, (not exceeding One Thousand Men in the whole) as shall be thought necessary to be employed in pursuing the Indian Enemy into their Places of Retreat, and Captivating them, their Wives and Children, was read, and compared, and on the Question whether the said Bill do pass or not? it was carried in the Affirmative.

<table>
  <tr>
    <th>Yeas.</th>
    <th>Yeas.</th>
    <th>Nays.</th>
    <th>Nays.</th>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Mr. Stevens,</td>
    <td>Mr. Vreeland,</td>
    <td>Mr. Holmes,</td>
    <td>Mr. Miller,</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Mr. Johnston,</td>
    <td>Mr. Vangeson,</td>
    <td>Mr. Smith,</td>
    <td>Mr. Learning,</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Mr. Nevill,</td>
    <td>Mr. Read,</td>
    <td>Mr. Nescobold,</td>
    <td>Mr. Spicer,</td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Mr. Dehart,</td>
    <td>Mr. Tard,</td>
    <td>Mr. Paxson,</td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Mr. Fisher,</td>
    <td>Mr. Middagh,</td>
    <td>Mr. Clement,</td>
    <td></td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>Mr. Hoggland,</td>
    <td></td>
    <td></td>
    <td></td>

Resolved,
That the said Bill do pass;
Ordered,
That Mr. Read, and Mr. Johnston do carry the said Bill to the Councell for their Concurrence.

The Draught of an Address to his Excellency, was read the second Time, and several Amendments being thereunto made, was agreed to by the House, and ordered to be engroisd.

The House adjourn'd till to-morrow morning nine o'Clock.
Saturday March 13 1756.

The House met.

Mr. Johnston reported, that Mr. Read and himself delivered the Bill with them entrusted to the Council.

The engrofs'd Address to his Excellency was readand compared.
Ordered,
That Mr. Speaker do Sign the same.
Ordered,
That Mr. Holmes, and Mr. Middah, do wait on his Excellency, and desire to know when he will be waited on by the House with their Address.

Mr. Holmes reported, that Mr. Middah, and himself delivered the above Message to his Excellency, who was pleased to say, the House might attend him as soon as they pleased.

Whereupon Mr. Speaker left the Chair, and with the House went to wait on his Excellency. And being returned, Mr. Speaker resumed the Chair, and reported, that the House had waited on his Excellency, and delivered their Address in the Words following.

To his Excellency JONATHAN BELCHER, Esq; Captain General and Governor in Chief, in and over his Majesty's Province of New-Jersey, and Territories thereon depending in America, Chancellor and Vice Admiral in the same, &c.

The Humble ADDRESS of the Representatives of the said Province in General Assembly met.

May it please your Excellency,

WE His Majesty's Dutiful and Loyal Subjects, the Representatives of the Colony of New-Jersey, in General Assembly conven'd, do think it our indispensible duty to join with your Excellency, in the most suitable acknowledgements to the King and Parliament, for their Care of the Colonies in North-America.

It is not unknown to your Excellency that this Colony hath at this Time seven Hundred and Fifty Men in pay, five Hundred of which assisted in the last Campaign with General Shirley, and we have never till lately made any doubt of their assisting throughout the whole of next Summer, but as the Officers recruiting under General Shirley, within this Government, have not only drained us of Men by considerable
Supplies of free Volunteers to the respective Regiments, but have also taken from us a large Number of Indented Servants and Apprentices, contrary to their Masters consent, and in violation of the Contracts subsisting between them; which last seems likely to involve this Colony in considerable Distresses, and may (it is to be feared) put us under the ungreatful necessity of not subsisting the said five Hundred Men, longer than the Money already provided for that Purpose will last; but if by a Majority of the House, it should be then concluded to make further Provision, we are very sure, considering our Circumstances and exertion of Strength in the common Cause, it is a Grievance we neither expected nor deserved, nor do we think if such a hardship, in all its ruinous Consequences to a young Country, were properly represented to our gracious Sovereign, it would be at all countenanced or encouraged.

As we have not the least doubt, but that if these Grievances are redressed, we shall continue the said five Hundred Men throughout the whole of another Campaign, so we hope upon a suitable Application from your Excellency, and the opinion we have hitherto entertained of General Shirley's Friendship, for the Colonies, we shall not fail of Redress in a Matter that so intimately concerns a great number of his Majesty's good Subjects.

We are obliged to keep a considerable Number of Troops for the Defence of our own Frontiers, and however cheerfully willing this House may be to promote the common Cause of the Colonies (of which we conceive no Part of our Conduct can suggest a doubt;) we are not able without an Inconvenience that bears no adequate Proportion to our Neighbours, and therefore must be excused from augmenting the Number of our Men in the manner proposed at the late Congress at New-York.

It gives us a very sensible Pleasure to hear that any of his Majesty's Colonies have entered into close Friendship and Alliance with the Indian Natives; an important Point, which tho' at first View of the greatest Consequence to the British Interest in America, we fear hath notwithstanding been too much neglected, and cannot but think the Cultivation of it by the Colonies concern'd, is worthy of their most serious Attention.

As to the Cries of our distressed Friends in this Colony, we are so far from endeavouring to withhold the use of their own Estates from them, as your Excellency is pleased to express it, that we have made their Cause the universal Concern of the Province, and what we apprehend is very extraordinary for their Protection, and we hope it will have its due weight with them, as it seems hitherto by the Di-
vine Blessing to have answered the Purposes of securing all the Persons who pay Taxes or look upon themselves as Inhabitants of this Colony.

By Order of the House.

Assembly-Chamber,
March 13, 1756.

ROBERT LAWRENCE Speaker.

Mr. Nevill had leave to be absent on urgent Business.

The House adjourn'd till two P. M.

The House met.

A Petition of Peter Donally was presented to the House, setting forth, that he went in one of the late Essex Companies for the Defence of the Frontiers at Delaware River, from whence he went with said Company for the relief of Daniel Depuy's Family and House in Pennsylvania, where he received a Wound in his Breast and Arm, whereby he is rendered unable to support himself, praying Support from this Colony, which was read, and ordered to lie on the Table.

The House Adjourn'd till Monday Morning nine o'Clock.

Monday March 15, 1756.

The House met.

Mr. Secretary read and laid before the House, an Account of his Disbursements of the Expences attending the Indian Treaty at Groswicks, in January last, and his Vouchers being produced and examined, whereby it appears that he hath expended the full of his Account, amounting to \( £49, 1s, 11d \).

Resolved,
That this House will make Provision for repaying the above Account, at the passing the next Bill for Support of Government.

The House adjourn'd till two P. M.

The House met.

Ordered,
That Mr. Fisher, and Mr. Paxson, do wait on the Council, and acquaint them, that this House have no Business before them, and being impatient to be dismissed, desire to know what Progress they have made in the Bill sent to them for Concurrence on Friday last.
Mr. Fisher reported, that Mr. Parson, and himself delivered the above Message to the Council.

A Message from the Council by Mr. Ashfield, acquainting this House that (in answer to their Message of this day) the Council have passed the Bill entitled an Act for raising Two hundred and Fifty able bodied Freemen as Volunteers, to protect and defend the Frontiers &c without any amendments.

Ordered, That Mr. Johnston and Mr. Stevens do wait on the Council and desire to know if they have any business before them, if not that they be prepared to apply to his Excellency for a Dismission.

Mr. Johnston reported that Mr. Stevens and himself delivered the above Messages to the Council, who say they have no Business before them.

Ordered, That Mr. Hancock and Mr. Fisher do wait on his Excellency and desire him to pass the Bill before him and to dismiss the House.

Mr. Hancock reported that Mr. Fisher and himself delivered the said Messages to his Excellency who was pleased to say he would dismiss the House to-morrow after he has considered the Bill, and make an answer to these Messengers of this House.

The House adjourned till to-morrow Nine O'Clock.
Tuesday March 16, 1756
The House met.

A Message from his Excellency, by his Clerk.

Mr. Speaker, his Excellency requires the attendance of the House in the Council Chamber immediately.

Whereupon Mr. Speaker left the Chair and with the House went to wait on his Excellency, who was pleased to give his assent to the following Bill, Enacting the same thing.

An Act for raising two hundred and fifty able bodied freemen as Volunteers, to protect & defend the Frontiers &c.

And then he made the following Speech by

Gentlemen of the General Assembly

"I have read over your Address in answer to my Speech, made to you at the opening of this Session, and wherein I state before you the present General and Particular State of this and the Neighbouring Colonies, and which I hoped would have induced you to have raised more Men than you have already for the Several Services Therein pointed out to you; but as you have not I shall be content and satisfied in having done my duty to his Majesty, as to the better protection and safety of these Colonies in General, and of this in particular.

"Take notice of the Complaint of the Officers recruiting under General Shirley, of their Intituting a large number of Indented Servants and Apprentices, and which you say you doubt not would be reprobated upon my making suitable application."
application to his Excellency General Shirley, and thus I am very ready to do in conjunction with your House, and to do it without delay.

Elizabeth Town Council Chamber
March 16 1756.

And then his Excellency adjourned this House to Wednesday the seventeenth day of April next to meet at Elizabeth Town.

Exam'd by David Brearley
Votes and Proceedings of the General Assembly of the Province of New Jersey At a Session held at Elizabeth Town began May 20, 1756 and continued to the 2nd day of June following
Votes of the General Assembly

Elizabeth Town Thursday May 20, 1756.

Pursuant to his Excellency's Adjournments of the General Assembly from time to time to this day the House met.

Ordered.

That Mr. Fisher and Mr. Kangeson do wait on his Excellency and acquaint him that a sufficient number of Members to proceed upon Business are met and are ready to receive anything he shall please to lay before them.

Mr. Fisher reported that Mr. Kangeson and himself delivered the above message to his Excellency, who has pleased to say the House should hear from him as soon as a Quorum of the Council were met.

The House adjourned till Tomorrow Morning 9 O'Clock

Friday May 21, 1756

The House met.
1. The message from his Excellency by Mr. Secretary Mr. Speaker, his Excellency is in the Council Chamber and requires the attendance of the House immediately.

Whereupon Mr. Speaker left the Chair, and with the House went to wait on his Excellency, and being returned Mr. Speaker resumed the Chair and reported that the House has waited on his Excellency, who was pleased to make a speech to the Council and House of Assembly of which Mr. Speaker said he had (to prevent mistakes) obtained a copy, which was ordered to be read, and it was read accordingly and is as follows.

"Gentlemen of the Council and of the General Assembly

"I am glad of this pleasing opportunity to congratulate you on the goodness and bounty of his Majesty, and his Parliament, in giving this Province a proportion of a large sum of money raised for several of his Majesty's Colonies in North America, as an acknowledgement of their past duty and services to his Majesty, and as an encouragement to them to behave with the like duty and loyalty for the future.

"The men that were to be raised by a late Act of this Province are fully completed, and are now proportioned and posted at the several Forts on the Frontiers, holding themselves in readiness to proceed elsewhere, as the King's service and the safety of the Province may require."
"According to the best advice, our Enemies the French are much straitened for Provisions in their several Settlements on this Continent, as well as in their Islands; I therefore earnestly recommend to you, the bringing forward as soon as may be, a Bill in strict Prohibition of the Exportation of all Sorts of Provisions and Warlike Stores from this Province and what the Neighbouring Government of New York has done of this kind, I shall be late before you.

Gentlemen of the General Assembly
It will be necessary that you carefully look into the Accounts of the Several Publick Officers of the Province, that you may see what Money are already expended and what more must be raised for the future Support of the Troops of this Province. By a Letter Inceived from Col: Schuyler (with a particular Account, which shall be delivered you) you will find his Regiment is in want of Money, Arms, & Cloathing and I shall not doubt of your immediate care to supply them.

As the Bill for the Support of the Government is expired you will do what may be necessary on this head for the future.

Gentlemen of the Council Of the General Assembly
I believe you will find upon a Revision, that several of your Temporary Laws are expired; which you may perhaps think necessary to be revived."
"As our Enemies are continually committing Hostilities against us; it is now more especially necessary for the King's Honour and Service, as well as for our own safety, to make some Amendments in the Militia Law, particularly to lay a Penalty on the Officers that do not do their duty in conformity to the Act now in being.

As many of your members have told me that this is a convenient Juncture for going through the Publick Affairs of the Province, I hope you will carefully and diligently engage in them, and soon bring this Session to a happy Conclusion?

I. Belchers
Council Chamber
Elizabeth Town May 21, 1756

Ordered.
That his Excellency's Speech have a second Reading in the Afternoon.

His Excellency by a Mr. Secretary laid before the House the several papers and Accounts referred to in his Speech, which were also read.

A Petition of Johannes Billesphelt and Peter Vanallen Foreigners, praying an Act for their Naturalization, was presented to the House, which was read and Ordered a Second reading.

The House adjourned till Two O'clock P.M."
The House met
His Excellency's Speech was read a second time, and committed to a Committee of the whole House.
The House adjourned till tomorrow morning 9 O'Clock
Saturday May 22: 1756

The House met
Mr. Secretary brought from his Excellency a written message to the House in the following words:
"Gentlemen of the General Assembly,
Early this Morning I received a Letter by Express from his Excellency the Governor of New York, which I herewith send you to shew you the absolute Necessity of your expediting a Bill for Prohibiting the Exports of Provisions."
I Belcher
Council Chamber May 22 1756

The House taking into consideration his Excellency's Message, with that part of his Speech relating to an Prohibition Act, are of Opinion that a Law for Prohibiting Provisions, is absolutely necessary at this Critical Juncture.

Ordered.
That Mr Spicer, Mr Johnston, Mr Fisher, Mr Stevens & Mr Wisherill be a Committee to prepare and bring in a Bill for the Purposes aforesaid.
Mr Speaker laid before the House a letter he had received from the Agent which was read.
The House adjourned till Monday Morning Nine O'clock

Monday May 24, 1750

The House met.

W' Secretary brought from his Excellency a Written Message as follows:

"Gentlemen of the General Assembly,

"Since my message to you for expediting a Bill in Prohibition of the Exportation of Provisions. I have received a copy of such an Act passed in Pennsylvania which Iherewith send you; and it is much of the same nature of that Passed in New York, so I hope you will lose no time in getting such a Bill through your House.

I. Belcher.

Council Chamber May 24, 1756

W' Spicer from the committee Appointed for that Purpose brought in a Bill entitled An Act for the more effectually obstructing the Exportation of Provisions and Warlike Stores from the Colony of New Jersey; which was read & ordered a second reading.

A Petition was presented to the House from Peter Salnave, setting forth that he having the Command of one of the State Troop Companies on the Frontiers, and going with his said Company to the Relief of Daniel Despuy's House and Family in Pennsylvania, had one of his Men named Peter Donally much Wounded; for the Cure and Transportation of whom, he had expended Sunday Sums
of Money, praying a Reimbursement thereof from this Colony, which was read and ordered a Second Reading.

A Petition was presented to the House from several of the Inhabitants of Elizabeth Town, setting forth that in the beginning of this present Expedition they had several Horses impressed and detained from them some length of time in His Majesty's Service for which they have received no Satisfaction, praying the House to make Provision for payment of their Damages thereby sustained, which was read and Ordered a Second Reading.

Several Petitions were presented to the House from the County of Morris signed by 190 hands, setting forth the hardships they labour under by having no Members allowed to Represent them in General Assembly praying the Legislature to grant them the usual Privileges as the other Counties enjoy in being Represented by two Members in General Assembly for the future, which were read and ordered a Second Reading.

The Bill entitled an Act for the more effectual obstructing the exportation of Provisions and Warlike Stores from the Colony of New Jersey was read the Second Time and Committed to a Committee of the whole House.

The House accordingly Resolved itself into a Committee of the whole House on the aforementioned Bills after some time spent therein Mr Speaker Resumed the Chair and
and Mr. Nevill Chairman of the Committee reported, that the Committee has gone through the said Bill and made several Amendments thereto, which by leave he read and delivered in at the Table, where the same being again read was agreed to by the House.

Ordered,
That the said Bill be Engraffed

On Motion Made, the Question was put, Whether the House will proceed on the common Business at this place, or desire his Excellency to adjourn the General Assembly to Perth Amboy for that Purpose? it was carried as follows

For Elizabeth Town, in the present difficult state of things
Mr. Lohart
Mr. Bradberry
Mr. Fisher
Mr. Hoggland
Mr. Ireland
Mr. Vangosen

For Amboy, as one of the usual places of doing Business
Mr. Read
Mr. Lord
Mr. Miller
Mr. Yard
Mr. Meldaghe
Mr. Stevens
Mr. Johnston
Mr. Nevill
Mr. Holmes
Mr. Smith
Mr. Newbold
Mr. Parsons
Mr. Hancock
Mr. Learning
Mr. Spicer

The House adjourned till Tomorrow Morning 9 O'clock
Tuesday May 25: 1756
The House met,

The engrossed Bill entitled An Act for the more effectual Obstructing the Exportation of Provisions and Warlike Stores from the Colony of New Jersey was read and concurred;
and on the Question:
Resolved Nominis Contradictoris
That the said Bill do Pass.

Ordered
That Mr. Read and Mr. Johnston do carry the said Bills to the Council for their Concurrence.

A Petition of Peter Donally, was presented to the House, setting forth that he went in one of the late Gasp Companies for the Defence of the Frontiers, from whence he went with said Company for the Relief of Daniel Depuy's House and Family in Pennsylvania where he received a wound in his Breast and Arm and thereby is rendered unable to support himself praying Support from the Colony, which was read and Ordered a Second Reading.

Mr. Miller by leave given brought in a Bill entitled an Act to enable the Proprietors of two Tracts of Tide Meadow and Marsh lying and adjoining on the North side of Cohansy Creek &c. to stop out the Tides &c. which was read and Ordered a Second Reading.

Mr. Hancock by leave given brought in a Bill entitled an Act to enable the Owners of some Meadows and Marshes at Elsonborough in the County of Salem, to keep out the Tides from Overflowing the same which was read and Ordered a Second Reading.
Mr. Johnston reported that Mr. Read and himself delivered the Ball with them unto the Council.

The House resolved itself into a Committee of the whole House on his Excellency's Speech. After some time spent therein Mr. Speaker resumed the Chair &c W. Hancock Chairman of the Committee reported that the Committee had made some progress in the matters to them referred and desired leave to set again. To which the House agreed.

The House adjourned till 2 P.M.

The House met.

The House again Resolved itself into a Committee of the whole House on his Excellency's Speech. After some time spent therein Mr. Speaker resumed the Chair &c W. Hancock Chairman of the Committee reported that the Committee had gone through his Excellency's Speech to them referred and had come to sundry Resolutions; which by leave he reported as follows.

Resolved,
That a humble Address be presented to his Excellency, in answer to his favourable Speech. To which the House agreed.

Resolved,
That $15,000 be struck in paper Bills of Credit, for the further subsistence and pay of the Troops of this Colony, under Col. Peter Schuyler until the 1st day of November next.
next, and also for subsisting and paying such of the Troops of this Colony, as may be found necessary to remain in Defence of our Frontiers, or that may be detached from thence in pursuance of the Indian Enemy into their places of Retreat, Provided a reasonable time can be obtained for Sinking the same. To which the House agreed.

<table>
  <tr>
    <th>Yeas</th>
    <th>Nays</th>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td>W Stevens</td>

    <td>W Smith</td>

    <td>W Miller</td>

    <td>W Spicers</td>
  </tr>

    <td></td>

    <td>W Ladd</td>
    <td></td>

Resolved
That his Majesty's Government of New Jersey be supported for One Year, to commence the 21st day of May 1796 and to expire the 21st day of May 1797. To which the House agreed.

Resolved
That the Acts of Assembly of this Colony that will expire by their own Limitations at the end of this Session of General Assembly, or soon after be continued as they now are for one Year, and from Thence to the end of the next Session.

Ordered
That W Smith, W Hancock, W Stevens, W Miller and W Maddagh be a Committee to prepare the Draught of
of the Address to his Excellency.

Ordered
That Mr. Read & Mr. Wetherill be a Committee to examine what Laws have expired, or are near expiring by their own Limitation and make report thereof to this House.

Ordered
That Mr. Smith, Mr. Leaming, Mr. Holmes, Mr. Nangeron and Mr. Roagland, be a Committee to bring in a Bill for Support of Government.

Ordered
That Mr. Spicer, Mr. Yard, Mr. Fisher, Mr. Newbolds & Mr. Ladd be a Committee to prepare and bring in a Bill for the Striking $15,000 in Bills of Credit for the subsistence and Pay of the Forces of this Colony.

The Petition of Johannes Belesvelt and Peter Nanallen, for Naturalization was read the Second Time.

Ordered
That the Petitioners have leave to bring in a Bill, agreeable to the Prayer of their Petition upon their taking the usual Oaths, and making and subscribing the Declaration according to Law.

The Bill entitled an Act to enable the Proprietors of two several Tracts of Tede Meadow & Marsh lying and adjoining on the North Side of Cohansy Creek &c. to stop out, The Title &c. was read the second time, and committed to Mr. Holmes and Mr. Spicer.
The Bill entitled an Act to Enable the Owners of some Meadows and Marches at Elsenborough in the Country of Salem to keep out the Tide from overflowing the same was read the Second Time, and Committed to Mr. Smith and Mr. Newbold.

Ordered:
"That Mr. Read, Mr. Spicer and Mr. Manning, assisted by Mr. Speaker do wait on His Excellency, and acquaint him that the House being willing to continue to supply the Forces of this Colony under Col. Schuyler but sensible of the additional heavy Burthen it will lay upon the Colony, are extremely desirous to have the Same of sending the Money postponed till the Province can have discharged the unprecedented Taxes already laid on it, and therefore to prevent unnecessary Delay and Trouble, would desire to be informed, whether he is at Liberty (the necessity of the Times concurred) to consent to an Act, for emitting a Sum of Paper Money with a longer time than heretofore to Sink it in."

The House adjourned till Tomorrow Morning 9 O'Clock

Wednesday May 26, 1756

The House met

Mr. Read reported that Mr. Spicer, Mr. Manning and himself assisted by Mr. Speaker, had delivered the Message of the House with them entrusted to His Excellency, who was pleased to say he would send the House a Message in answer thereto.
Mr. Smith from the Committee, to whom was committed the Bill entitled an Act to enable the Owners of some Meadows and Marshes at Elsenborough in the County of Salern to keep out the Tide &c., reported the same without any amendments; which Bill being again read was agreed to by the House.

Ordered
That the said Bill be engrossed.

Mr. Spicer from the Committee to whom was committed the Bill entitled An Act to enable the Proprietors of two Several Tracts of Tide Meadow and Marsh lying and Adjoining on the North side of Chansoy Creek &c. to stop out the Tide &c. reported the same without any amendments, which bill being again read was agreed to by the House.

Ordered
That the said Bill be engrossed

It appearing to this House that Johannes Belesvelt, & Peter Kanallen have taken the usual Oaths, and made and subscribed the Declaration Prescribed by Law; Whereupon (M. Vangison agreeable to leave given in their behalfs Brought in a Bill Entitled an Act, for Naturalizing Johannes Belesvelt & Peter Kanallen; which was read and Ordered a Second reading.

Mr. Smith from the Committee Appointed for that purpose
purpose brought in a Bill for the Support of Government, which was read and ordered a Second Reading.

Mr. Spicer from the Committee appointed for that purpose, brought in a Bill for the Striking $15,000 in Bills of Credit, for the Subsistence and Pay of the Forces of this Colony &c. which was read and Ordered a Second Reading.

The engrossed Bill entitled An Act to enable the Owners of some Meadows & Marshes at Ebenborough in the County of Salem to keep out the Tides &c. was read and compared, and on the Question

Resolved
That the said Bill do pass
Ordered
That Mr. Read and Mr. Hancock do carry the said Bill to the Council for concurrence.

The engrossed Bill entitled An Act to enable the Proprietors of two several Tracts of Tide Meadow and Marsh lying and adjoining on the North side of Cohansy Creek &c. to keep out the Tides &c. was read and compared, and on the Question

Resolved
That the said Bill do Pass.
Ordered
That Mr. Read and Mr. Hancock do carry the said Bill to the Council for concurrence.
The Bill entitled An Act for Naturalizing Johannes Belevvelt & Peter Vanallen was read the Second Time and committed to Mr Yard & W Dehart.

The House adjourned till 2 P.M

The House met.

A Written Message from his Excellency by Mr Secretary Gentlemen of the General Assembly.

"I have considered your Message of Yesterday, and as I always have done, so I shall continue to do everything in my power to make his Majesty's good people of this Province easy. But to consent to an Act for emitting a sum of Paper Currency, in a longer time than heretofore to sink it, would be in such Breach of the King's Royal Orders to me in that Behalf, as well as a Neglect of Letters I have received from the Right Honourable the Lord Commissioners for Trade & Plantations, that I dare not presume to give my assent to a Bill for issuing any paper currency, so as to protract the time for drawing in the same beyond what has been practised in the late Conventions. But if the Council & Assembly are minded to make application to his Majesty for lengthening out the Funds for any future Emigrations, I shall readily give my assistance in writing to the King's Ministers favourably in case to the King's good Subjects of this Province, on this head.

Elizabeth Towns
Council Chamber May 26, 1756

I. Belcher."
11th Yard from the Committee to whom was committed the Bill entitled An Act for Naturalizing Johannes Belevvelt and Peter Vanathen reported the same without any amendments, and the same being again read, was agreed to by the House.

Ordered.
That the said Bill be engross'd.

A Petition from the Prisoners for Debt in Trenton Goals was presented to the House, praying to be released from their Confinement; which was read & referred to further Consideration.

The Bill for Support of Government, was read the Second Time, and committed to a Committee of the whole House.

The Petition from several of the Inhabitants of Elizabeth Town for Satisfaction of the Damages they Sustained by having their Horses impress'd and detained in his Majesty's Service, was read the Second Time & referred to the Committee on the Bill for Support of Government.

An Account was laid before the House from the Board of Justices & Freeholders of Hunterdon County containing the Charges of Confining and Supporting several Indians in Trenton Goal; which was read and referred to the Committee on the Bill for Support of Government.

An Account was laid before the House from Joshua Bishpham Esq' of Burlington County, relating to his Tenuring the Qualifications Prescribed in His Excellency's Proclamation.
Proclamation, to several Indians, and furnishing them with the Badge Certificates &c. which was read, and referred to the Committee on the Bill for Support of Government.

The House resolved itself into a committee of the whole House on the Bill for Support of Government &c. After some time spent therein, Mr. Speaker resumed the Chair, and Mr. Neville, Chairman of the Committee reported that the Committee had gone through the said Bill and come to several Resolutions, which by leave he reported and delivered in at the Table where the said Resolutions (with the said Bill) being again read, and were as follows:

Resolved.
That in and by said Bill when passed into a Law, there be paid to His Excellency Jonathan Belcher Esq. One Thousand Pounds per annum. And on the Question whether the House agree thereto or not? it passed in the Affirmative.

Yeas
Mr. Stevens
Mr. Nevill

Mr. Kangerson

Mr. Spicer

To Samuel Nevill Esq. Second Justice of the Supreme Court, Twenty five Pounds per annum.
To Richard Salter third Justice of the Supreme Court Twenty five pounds per annum.

To Governor's Skinner Esq Attorney General of this Colony Thirty pounds per annum.

To Andrew Johnston and Samuel Smith Esq Treasurers of this Colony Forty pounds per annum each.

To Charles Read Esq Clerk of the Council Thirty pounds per annum.

To Richard Patridge Esq Agent of this Colony Eighty pounds per annum.

To John Longth Esq Clerk of the Council of this Colony Twenty pounds per annum.

To the Doorkeeper of the Council Ten Pounds per annum.

To Jonathan Butcher Esq Sixty Pounds per annum for House Rent, and on the Question whether the House Agrees thereto or not; it passed in the affirmative.

Yeas
Mr. Stevens
Mr. Nevill
Mr. Dehart
Mr. Bradberry
Mr. Fisher
Mr. Hoagland
Mr. Wideland
Mr. Vangerson

Nays
Mr. Read
Mr. Smith
Mr. Hancock
Mr. Miller
Mr. Leaming
Mr. Yard
Mr. Middagh

To the Chief Justice, or any one of the Justices of the Supreme Court for attending Circuit Courts, and Courts of Oyer and Terminer in the manner prescribed by said Act, the sum of Ten pounds each Time.
To each of the Counsels the same they have respectively attended or shall attend, at this or any other setting of General Assembly within the term aforesaid, Six shillings per Diem.

To the Clerks of the House of Representatives, for their attendance the sum of Eight Shillings per Diem; also four pence per sheet reckoning Ninety Words to the Sheet, for entering the Minutes fair in the Book, and copying the Laws and Minutes for the Printer, also to Abraham Clark Junr., one of the Clerks, Eight Pounds for Pen, Ink and Paper at this and former sittings.

To the Secretary for Charges attending Expresses to this Town Eighteen pounds twelve Shillings, and in full of the charges attending the late Indian Treaty at Creswick Fifty Pounds Eleven Shillings and Eleven pence.

To each of the Treasurers of this Colony, six pence per pound for exchanging ragged and torn Bills of Credit.

To James Parker or any other Printer, for Printing the Minutes of the House, during the continuance of this Act; and to William Bradford for Printing the Laws passed at this or any other Setting as aforesaid; such Sums as Charles Read, Hendrick Fisher, John Johnston, Henry Papson and John Ladd Esq." or any two of them shall agree to be paid them for the said Services.

To the Sergeant-at-Arms for the Time being who hath or shall attend the House of Representatives during the said Term. The sum of Three Shillings per Diem.
To the Sergeants at Arms for the Time being who hath or shall attend the Council as aforesaid, the Sum of three Shillings per Diem.

To the Doorkeeper of the House of Representatives for the Times being who hath or shall attend in manner aforesaid the Sum of three Shillings & six pence per Diem.

To Joshua Bispham Esq. in full of his Account for registering and providing Badges for the Indians Six pounds.

To the Sheriff of Hunterdon in full of his Account for maintaining an Indian Prisoner. Seven pounds two Shillings and Eight pence.

To Samuel Woodruff Esq. in full of his Account for Badges for Indians and other Expenses. Seven pounds two Shillings and three pence.

To Petrus Decker Esq. for himself & Associates for escorting some Indians to Elizabeth Town. Five pounds Eight Shillings.

To Jacob Dehart to pay Mary Dennis the sum of Twenty Shillings per Week for the use of a Room, Fire Woods and Candles for the House during this and the late sittings of Assembly's.

To Andrew Johnson Esq. the sum of Fifteen Shillings per Week to pray for the Use of a Room, Fire Woods and Candles for the Council during this or any other sitting within the said Time.

To each of the Members of the House of Representatives for their attendance at this or any other Sitting during
dividing the said Town, the Town of New Burlington &c &c.

To all which several towns the House agreed and on the Question whether the said Bill be engrossed or not, it passed in the Affirmative.

Ordered
That the said Bill be engrossed.

A Message from the Council by W' Abbott's account informing the House that the Council have passed the Bill intitled An Act for the more effectual Constraining the Exportation of Provisions &c with an amendment thereto, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.

The House taking into Consideration the Amendment made by the Council to the above Bill, and on the Question whether the House agree to the said Amendment or not, it was carried in the Affirmative.

Ordered
That the said Bill as amended be re-engrossed.

Mr. Read reported that W' Hancock and himself delivered the two Bills with them entrusted to the Council.

The House Adjourned till Tomorrow morning at 9 O'clock

Tuesday May 27, 1756

The House met.

The Bill intitled An Act for the more effectual Constraining the Exportation of Provisions and Warlike Stores from the Colony of New Jersey, re-engrossed with the Council's Amendment
Amendment being read and compared, and on the Question.

Resolved,
That the same do pass

Ordered,
That W. Fisher & M'Newbold do carry the same to the Council.

The Bill intitled An Act for making current 1800 &c was read the second time and committed to a committee of the whole House.

The House accordingly resolved itself into a committee of the whole House to proceed on the Bill for making current 1800 &c After some time spent therein Mr Speaker resumed the Chair, and Mr Hancock (Chairman of the Committee) reported that the Committee had made some progress in the matters to them referred, and desired leave to sit again, to which the House agreed.

Mr Read from the Committee reported to instruct what laws have expired or are now expiring, reported that the following Laws will expire the end of this session, or soon after viz:

An Act to prevent Actions of Trespass brought &c under being brought into the Supreme Court of this Province.
An Act to erect and establish Courts in the several Counties of this Colony for the Tryal of small causes.
An Act for the better enabling of Creditors to recover their just Debts from Persons who Obscure themselves, and
An Act for better Settling and regulating the Militia.
of this Colony of a new Levy, for the repelling Invasions, and suppressing Insurrections and Rebellion.

Ordered:
That Mr. Neill and Mr. Spicer do bring in a Bill to further continue the said Laws.

Mr. Neill accordingly brought in a Bill entitled An Act for continuing several Acts &c. which was read, and ordered a Second Reading.

Address from His Excellency by Mr. Secretary

Gentlemen of the General Assembly,

I have this morning received by Express a Letter and an Appendix giving very melancholy Accounts from the Frontiers which the Secretary by my order shall lay before you. But I am very happy that the Legislature is now sitting, that immediate Measures may be taken for the better Protection of the Colony on which it is now apparent the Savage Enemy have commenced their Barbarities. It is absolutely necessary that a Law should pass for the Regulating of the Forces stationed there, and that Suitable Provision should be made for a Commander in Chief, as may engage some person of Reputation and Abilities to accept of the Command, whereby these troops will be rendered vastly more usefull. If these Inroads continues you will easily conceive that Destruction and Devastation must follow.

A. Butler
Council Chamber May 2nd, 1756
The House adjourned till two P.M.

The House met.

The Bill entitled An Act for continuing several Acts of Assembly &c was read the second time and committed to Mr. Ladd & Mr. Newbold.

The House again resolved itself into a Committee of the whole House on the Bill for making Patent $3000 &c after some time spent therein Mr. Speaker resumed the Chair, and Mr. Hancock Chairman of the Committee reported, that the Committee had gone through the said Bill, and made several Amendments thereto which he is ready to report when the House will receive the same.

Ordered
That the Report be made Tomorrow Morning

A Message from his Excellency by Secretary
Gentlemen of the General Assembly
"to have just now received repeated Accounts of
the Murders and Depredations committed by the enemy
in the County of Sussex. I now send them to you for
your deliberate consideration; that something may be
done before this Season is over, for the more effectual
Protection and Defences of the Poor people on the
Frontiers, or I am afraid Distress will even consume
the Province as an Armed Man!"

Journal Chamber at May 27, 1756 J. Belcher
Mr. Foster reported that McNewbold and himself delivered the Bill with them entrusted to the Council.

Mr. Ladd from the Committee to whom was committed the Bill entitled An Act for continuing several Acts of Assembly. He reported the said Bill without any amendments, and that same being read again was agreed to by the House.

Ordered
That the said Bill be engrossed

The engrossed Bill entitled an Act for Support of Government etc. was read and compared; and on the Question

Resolved
That the said Bill do pass.

Ordered
That Mr. Read & Mr. Smith do carry the said Bill to the Council for Concurrence.

The engrossed Bill entitled An Act for Naturalizing Johannes Belweth & Peter Vanallton was read and compared and on the Question

Resolved
That the said Bill do pass.

Ordered
That Mr. Freeland and Mr. Vangewondo carry the said Bill to the Council for Concurrence.

A Message from the Council by Mr. Ashfield acquainting this
The House that the Council have passed the Bill entitled An Act to Enable the Owners of some Meadows and Marshes at Rosenborough in the County of Salem to keep out the Tides from Overflowing the same without any amendment.

Mr. Smith reported that Mr. Read himself delivered the Bill with them entrusted to the Speaker in Council.

Mr. Vangerson reported that Mr. Kidland and himself delivered the Bill with them entrusted to the Council.

The House Adjourned till Tomorrow morning 9 O'clock

Friday May 28, 1756

The House met.

The engrossed Bill entitled An Act for continuing several Acts of the General Assembly of this Colony, which will expire at the end of this present Session, was read and Compared, and on the Question:

Resolved,
That the said Bill do pass.

Ordered,
That Mr. Holmes and Mr. Miller de carry the said Bill to Council for Concurrence.

A Petition was presented to the House from sundry of the Inhabitants of the Counties of Gloucester & Harlenton, setting forth the Damages they frequently sustain by having their Swine killed by Beares occasioning
An Act for giving rewards to such as may kill many &c which was read and referred to further consideration when the House shall meet at Burlington.

A petition was presented to the House from Sunday of the Inhabitants of the County of Salem, living near the River Delaware, setting forth the danger they are exposed to from the Enemy, in case of a war with France, praying that a proper watch may be appointed and supported by the Province, and placed along the said River, to give information if any of the Enemy's Vessels or Forces should appear in said River, which was read and referred to further consideration.

A message from the Council by Mr. Ashfield, acquainting the House that the Council have passed the Bill entitled An Act for Naturalizing Johannes Beleswell and Peter Vanalton without any amendment.

Mr. Holmes reported that Mr. Miller and himself delivered the bill with them entrusted to the Council.

Ableable to the order of yesterday W. Hantock Chairman of the Committee to whom was committed the Bill for making Current $15000 &c reported the said Bill with the amendments made by the Committee there to being now Entitled an Act for Making Current £15000 in Bills of Credit for the further supply and Pay of the Forces lately raised in this Colony under the command of Col. Peter Schuyler and also for subscribing and paying such of the Troops of this Colony as
as may be found necessary to remain in defence of our Frontiers or that may be detached from thence on pursuing the Indian Enemy into their Places of Retreat, and Three Thousand five hundred Pounds more for exchanging ragged and Torn Bills; and for directing the manner how the £17500 shall be sunk, which said Bill as aforesaid being then was agreed to by the House.

Ordered
The said Bill be engrossed

Ordered
W. Head
That W. Stevens and W. Fisher be a Committee to bring in a Bills for regulating the Forces on the Frontiers of this Colony &c. and making further Provision for their Pay

The House adjourn'd till Two P.Ms
The House met.

Ordered
That the Commissioners, Companies and other Publick officers concerned in laying out the Colony for the Present and late Expeditions do lay their Accounts before the House the first day of the next sitting for doing the Publick business of the Colony.

Mr Jonith from the Committee appointed for that purpose brought in the Draught of an Address to his Excellency which was twice read, and agreed to by the House and ordered to be engrossed.
The engrossed Address to his Excellency was read and compared.

Ordered
That the Speaker do sign the same?

Ordered
That Mr. Spicer and Mr. Stevens do wait on his Excellency and desire to know when he will be waited on by the House with their Address.

Mr. Stevens reported that Mr. Spicer and himself had waited on his Excellency with the Message of the House, who was pleased to say he would receive the Address tomorrow.

The several Petitions from the County of Morris, Praying for Liberty to choose Members of Assembly were read the Second Time, and referred to the next sitting of the Assembly at Burlington.

The House taking into Consideration an Account of Doctor Barnhill for Service done to Peter Donally one of the late Troops on the Frontiers who was wounded in Pennsylvania

Resolved
That it is the Opinion of this House the Commissary ought to pay for his Services and Medicine on that occasion the sum of $13.2.14 Proclamation Money.

The Petition of Peter Solmavere was read the second time.

Resolved
That it is the Opinion of this House that the Commissary
ought to adjust his accounts, and pay so much as shall be found to be just.

The Petition of Peter Donally was read the second time.

Resolved,
That it is the Opinion of this House, that if the Commissary allows him the sum of Ten Pounds Proclamation, as an amendment for the loss of Time, by being wounded, it will be allowed him in his Accounts.

The House adjourned for half an hour.

The House met.

10. Read from the committee appointed for that purpose brought in a Bill entitled An act for the better regulation of the Forces on the Frontiers &c., which was read and ordered a Second Reading.

The House adjourned till to-morrow Morning 9 O'clock
Saturday May 29, 1756.

The House Met.

A Petition was presented to the House from New Town in Sussex County, praying for Protection against the savage Indians, which was read and referred to further consideration.

A message from the Council by Wc Ashfield, acquainting the House, that the Council have passed the Bill for Support of Government without any amendment.
